content.text: Why Telemetry Schemas Need Governance

A telemetry schema is the contract between the services that emit metrics, logs, and traces and the dashboards, alerts, and automated systems that consume them. When a service renames a metric, removes a tag, or changes a tag value format, dashboards silently break — queries return empty results, SLO calculations shift, and on-call engineers see alerts that fire or fail to fire incorrectly. Without governance, schema changes are deployed opportunistically and breakage is discovered in production.

GenAI services amplify this risk because their telemetry schemas are newer, less standardised, and evolve faster than typical web service schemas. The model name field changes every time a new model is released, token counting semantics differ across providers, and the safety classifier output format changes when the underlying model is updated. A governance process turns these changes from surprises into planned migrations.
The core rule:

No telemetry consumer — dashboard, alert, SLO calculation, cost report, or automated pipeline — should break without a deprecation notice. Any schema change that would break a consumer is a breaking change and requires a migration window.
Schema Definition Format

A telemetry schema document is the authoritative definition of every metric, span attribute, and log field the service emits. It lives in version control alongside the service code, follows semantic versioning, and is the first file that changes when instrumentation is modified.

# genai-telemetry-schema.yaml
# Authoritative schema for all telemetry emitted by the chat-api service.
# Every PR that changes instrumentation must update this file.

schema_version: "v2.1"
service: chat-api
owner: platform-observability
last_updated: "2025-06-01"
reviewers:
  - alice@example.com   # observability platform lead
  - bob@example.com    # SRE on-call rotation

metrics:
  - name: genai_chat_request_total
    type: counter
    description: "Total chat completion requests received"
    unit: "1"
    tags:
      required:
        - name: provider
          values: [openai, anthropic, google, self-hosted]
        - name: model
          description: "Exact model name as sent in the request"
        - name: env
          values: [production, staging, development]
        - name: service
          description: "Service name"
        - name: region
          description: "Cloud region"
      optional:
        - name: finish_reason
          values: [stop, length, content_filter, tool_calls, unknown]
        - name: tier
          values: [free, pro, enterprise]
        - name: stream
          values: ["true", "false"]

  - name: genai_chat_request_duration_seconds
    type: histogram
    description: "End-to-end chat completion request duration"
    unit: "s"
    histogram_buckets: [0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 8.0, 12.0, 20.0, 30.0, 60.0, 120.0]
    tags:
      required:
        - name: provider
        - name: model
        - name: env
        - name: service
        - name: region
      optional:
        - name: finish_reason
        - name: stream

spans:
  - name: "gen_ai chat"
    kind: CLIENT
    required_attributes:
      - gen_ai.system
      - gen_ai.request.model
      - gen_ai.usage.input_tokens
      - gen_ai.usage.output_tokens
    recommended_attributes:
      - gen_ai.response.model
      - gen_ai.response.finish_reasons
      - gen_ai.response.id
      - gen_ai.request.max_tokens
Semantic Versioning for Telemetry Schemas

Semantic versioning (semver) applied to telemetry schemas gives consumers a machine-readable signal about the nature of a change. The major version increments on breaking changes; the minor version increments on backward-compatible additions. Consumers can assert a minimum minor version without breaking when new fields are added.
The Schema Review Process

Schema changes go through a review process modelled on an API RFC. The process exists because telemetry consumers are distributed across many teams and repositories — a change that looks trivial to the emitting team can break production monitoring for three other teams.
File a schema PR.

The service team opens a pull request that changes the schema YAML and the instrumentation code together. The PR description must classify the change (breaking / non-breaking) and list which dashboards, alerts, and SLO calculations are affected.
Automated lint runs.

A CI job validates the schema against the naming standards, checks that all required fields are present, verifies the version number was bumped correctly, and flags any forbidden high-cardinality tags.
Observability platform review.
The platform team reviews breaking changes for migration feasibility. They verify that a dual-emission window has been planned and that migration documentation is complete.
Consumer impact assessment.

The platform team queries the metrics backend to enumerate active dashboards and alerts that use the affected metric names or tag keys. Each consumer must be migrated before the old schema is retired.
Approval and version assignment.
The platform team approves the PR and the schema version is committed. The corresponding SDK version is pinned in the release notes.
Breaking Change Migration: Dual Emission

When a breaking schema change is approved, services must dual-emit — they emit both the old and new metric formats simultaneously for a migration window of at least 90 days. This gives all consumers time to migrate their queries, dashboards, and alert rules without a forced cut-over.

from opentelemetry import metrics
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class DualEmitContext:
    """
    Emits both old and new metric names/tags simultaneously during a
    migration window. Remove old_* instruments after the migration deadline.
    """
    # Schema v1 instruments (deprecated — remove after 2025-09-01)
    old_request_counter: metrics.Counter
    old_token_counter: metrics.Counter

    # Schema v2 instruments (current)
    new_request_counter: metrics.Counter
    new_token_counter: metrics.Counter

    migration_deadline: str = "2025-09-01"


def record_request_dual_emit(
    ctx: DualEmitContext,
    provider: str,
    model: str,
    env: str,
    service: str,
    region: str,
    input_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    finish_reason: str,
) -> None:
    # v1 schema: used "llm_provider" and "llm_model" as tag names (legacy)
    old_tags = {
        "llm_provider": provider,   # old name
        "llm_model": model,         # old name
        "environment": env,         # old name for "env"
        "svc": service,             # old abbreviated name
    }
    ctx.old_request_counter.add(1, old_tags)
    ctx.old_token_counter.add(input_tokens + output_tokens, old_tags)

    # v2 schema: standardised gen_ai.* tag names
    new_tags = {
        "provider": provider,
        "model": model,
        "env": env,
        "service": service,
        "region": region,
        "finish_reason": finish_reason,
    }
    ctx.new_request_counter.add(1, new_tags)
    ctx.new_token_counter.add(input_tokens, {**new_tags, "token_type": "input"})
    ctx.new_token_counter.add(output_tokens, {**new_tags, "token_type": "output"})
Automated Schema Drift Detection

Services drift from their declared schemas when instrumentation is updated without updating the schema document, or when a dependency upgrade changes tag values silently. An automated drift detector compares live metric emissions against the declared schema and pages when they diverge.
Schema Registry as a Service

At scale — dozens of services, hundreds of metrics — a schema registry provides a queryable API for schema discovery, consumer enumeration, and migration tracking. Services register their schemas at deploy time; the registry tracks versions and flags services still on deprecated schema versions.
Log Schema Governance

Structured logs carry their own schema concerns. A log schema defines the fields, types, and allowed values for each log event type. For GenAI services the most important log schema is the request log — it records enough context to reconstruct what happened in an incident without exposing PII or prompt content.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import json
import hashlib


@dataclass
class GenAIChatRequestLog:
    """
    Structured log record for a chat completion request.
    Schema version: v1.2
    PII rule: prompt and completion text are NEVER logged here.
              Use a separate secure audit log with restricted access.
    """
    schema_version: str = "v1.2"
    trace_id: str = ""
    span_id: str = ""

    # Identity (low cardinality — safe to log)
    service: str = ""
    provider: str = ""
    model: str = ""
    env: str = ""
    region: str = ""

    # Request context (no free text)
    route: str = ""
    tier: str = ""
    feature: str = ""
    prompt_template_id: str = ""
    prompt_template_version: str = ""

    # Outcome
    duration_ms: float = 0.0
    input_tokens: int = 0
    output_tokens: int = 0
    finish_reason: str = ""
    cost_usd: float = 0.0
    error_type: Optional[str] = None
    http_status: int = 200

    def to_json_line(self) -> str:
        return json.dumps(self.__dict__, default=str)

    @staticmethod
    def validate(record: dict) -> list[str]:
        required = {"trace_id", "service", "provider", "model",
                    "env", "input_tokens", "output_tokens", "finish_reason"}
        return [f"missing field: {f}" for f in required if f not in record]
Deprecation Runbook
When a metric, tag, or log field is deprecated, execute this runbook in sequence to avoid consumer breakage.
Announce deprecation.
Post to the observability team Slack channel and the engineering all-hands. Include the metric name, the replacement (if any), and the sunset date — at least 90 days from the announcement.
Add deprecation annotation to the schema.
Mark the metric or field as deprecated in the schema YAML with the sunset date and replacement pointer. The CI linter emits a warning for any service that imports the deprecated field.
Enumerate consumers.
Query the metrics backend for every dashboard panel, alert rule, and recording rule that references the deprecated metric. Produce a spreadsheet with team owner, contact, and migration status.
Provide migration tooling.
If the change is a rename, provide a PromQL recording rule that bridges old and new names during the transition window. If it is a tag rename, provide an example dashboard panel migration.
Track migration progress.
Update the consumer spreadsheet weekly. Two weeks before sunset, page teams that have not migrated.
Sunset.
Remove dual emission from the service. Update the schema to mark the metric as removed. Archive the deprecation notice in the schema history file.
Summary

Telemetry schema governance treats the observability contract with the same discipline as an API contract. The schema lives in version control, follows semantic versioning, and changes through a review process that classifies breaking versus non-breaking modifications. Breaking changes require a dual-emission migration window of at least 90 days so that all consumers — dashboards, alerts, SLO calculations, and cost reports — can migrate without production incidents. Drift detection closes the loop by alerting when live emissions diverge from the declared schema. For GenAI services, where model names, token semantics, and safety classifier outputs change frequently, this governance is what makes observability stable across the rapid pace of model and provider evolution.
